title: Movement
description: Phrasal verbs about coming, going, and moving around

points.definition: To enter a room, building, or space

examples.english: Come in! The door is open.
She came in and sat down quietly.

points.notes: Very common greeting when someone knocks at the door.

points.definition: To leave your home for social activities or entertainment

examples.english: We go out every Friday night.
Do you want to go out for dinner tonight?

points.notes: Can also mean to leave any place: 'Go out of the room'.

examples.english: Get in the car. We're late!
The taxi arrived and we got in.

points.notes: Use 'get in' for small vehicles (car, taxi). Use 'get on' for large vehicles (bus, train, plane).

examples.english: We got out of the taxi at the station.
Get out of the car slowly.

points.notes: Opposite of 'get in'. Use 'get off' for buses, trains, planes.

examples.english: What time will you come back?
She came back from holiday last week.

points.notes: Similar to 'go back' but 'come back' implies returning to the speaker's location.

points.title: Practice: Movement
points.description: Complete the exercises with come in, go out, get in, get out, and come back

questions.feedback: Correct! 'Get in' is used for entering small vehicles.

questions.sentence: It's a beautiful evening. Let's ______ ______ for a walk.
questions.feedback: Great! 'Go out' means to leave home for an activity.

questions.question: You use 'get in' for which type of vehicle?
questions.feedback: Right! Use 'get in/out' for small vehicles, 'get on/off' for large ones.

questions.sentence: Please come ______, the meeting is about to start.
questions.feedback: 'Come in' means to enter a room or building.

questions.context: A: Where's your sister? B: She went out, but she'll ______ ______ soon.
questions.feedback: Correct! 'Come back' means to return to a place.

questions.answer: She got out of the car
questions.feedback: Perfect: 'She got out of the car'.
